channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Overview of the Italian Quantum Ecosystem
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive overview of Italy's quantum ecosystem offers valuable insights into one of Europe's most rapidly developing quantum programs, which has been gaining momentum through significant government investments and academic partnerships. Italy's strategic positioning in quantum technologies, including its participation in major EU quantum initiatives and growing startup ecosystem, makes this analysis particularly timely as the country emerges as a key player alongside quantum leaders like Germany and the Netherlands. The report from Politecnico di Milano's Quantum Computing & Communication Observatory provides crucial context for understanding how national quantum strategies are evolving across Europe and competing globally with initiatives from the US and China. For investors and policymakers, this deep dive into Italy's quantum landscape reveals both opportunities and challenges in a market that's becoming increasingly important for technological sovereignty and economic competitiveness.

Guest post by Director Marina Natalucci, Researcher Beatrice Goretti, and Researcher Margherita Moroni of the Quantum Computing & Communication Observatory at Politecnico di Milano In Italy, the quantum technology industry is going through a phase of rapid development, attracting growing international attention. Understanding the dynamics behind this growth is increasingly relevant for policymakers, investors, and [...] The post Overview of the Italian Quantum Ecosystem appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IBM Quantum Integrates Bivariate Bicycle Formulations with Algebraic Outer Concatenation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights IBM's continued push toward practical fault-tolerant quantum computing by combining advanced error correction techniques that could dramatically improve code efficiency. The integration of quantum low-density parity-check codes with algebraic concatenation represents a sophisticated approach to achieving the high error correction rates needed for useful quantum algorithms while minimizing qubit overhead. These theoretical advances in error correction architectures are crucial stepping stones toward IBM's ambitious fault-tolerance roadmap, potentially bringing commercially viable quantum computers closer to reality. While the technical details are complex, this work addresses one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: protecting quantum information from decoherence long enough to perform meaningful calculations.

An overview of the moving parts of the construction and analysis. IBM Quantum has expanded its fault-tolerant roadmap by reporting a unified structural synthesis that bridges high-rate quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes with algebraic outer block constraints. Announced via an architectural briefing by Director of IBM Research Jay Gambetta, the corporate workflow integrates two key [...] The post IBM Quantum Integrates Bivariate Bicycle Formulations with Algebraic Outer Concatenation appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IonQ Experimental Demonstration of Breakeven qLDPC and Block Codes on a Trapped-Ion Architecture
item.description:
Curator's Take: This experimental milestone from IonQ represents a significant step toward practical quantum error correction by demonstrating "breakeven" performance where error correction actually reduces rather than amplifies errors. The ability to run nine different quantum error-correcting codes on a single 40-qubit trapped-ion processor without hardware reconfiguration showcases remarkable versatility and suggests these systems are approaching the threshold where error correction becomes beneficial rather than burdensome. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the inclusion of quantum low-density parity-check codes, which are considered promising candidates for scaling to the thousands of qubits needed for fault-tolerant quantum computing. This work provides crucial experimental validation that multiple error correction approaches can coexist and perform effectively on near-term quantum hardware, bringing us closer to the era of truly reliable quantum computation.

Hardware developer IonQ, Inc. has reported the simultaneous experimental execution of nine distinct quantum error-correcting codes across three structural families—quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes, topological codes, and concatenated codes—compiled onto a single, non-reconfigured trapped-ion processor. Detailed in a technical manuscript deposited on the open-access arXiv repository, the research team utilized a linear chain of forty [...] The post IonQ Experimental Demonstration of Breakeven qLDPC and Block Codes on a Trapped-Ion Architecture appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Establishing an Analytical Framework to Benchmark Logical Qubit Performance Claims
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Alice & Bob white paper addresses a critical need in quantum computing as companies increasingly announce logical qubit milestones without standardized metrics for comparison. The proposed five-criteria framework could help the field move beyond marketing headlines to meaningful technical assessments, similar to how standardized benchmarks transformed classical computing performance comparisons. As we enter the era of early logical qubits across multiple platforms, having rigorous analytical standards becomes essential for tracking genuine progress toward fault-tolerant quantum computing. This work represents an important step toward establishing the measurement discipline needed to validate whether logical qubit implementations truly surpass the "break-even" threshold where error correction provides net benefit.

Over time the probability of measuring the correct result decreases. As in this illustrative example, the error correctionis "beyond breakeven" when the logical qubit decays more slowly than the best-performing constituent physical qubit. Researchers at Alice & Bob have published a white paper that proposes five criteria to benchmark logical qubit claims. Here is a [...] The post Establishing an Analytical Framework to Benchmark Logical Qubit Performance Claims appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Experimental Implementation of a Bucket-Brigade QRAM Architecture on a Superconducting Processor
item.description:
Curator's Take: This experimental demonstration of bucket-brigade QRAM on superconducting hardware represents a crucial step toward solving one of quantum computing's most persistent bottlenecks: efficiently loading classical data into quantum systems. The Zhejiang University team's work in Nature Physics tackles the fundamental challenge that quantum algorithms requiring large datasets face, where data preparation can consume exponential time and negate quantum advantages. While early-stage, this hardware implementation moves QRAM from theoretical concept to physical reality, potentially unlocking practical applications for quantum machine learning and database search algorithms that have long been limited by data input constraints. The research demonstrates how circuit-based approaches can begin addressing the scalability challenges that will be essential for quantum computers to process real-world datasets effectively.

Experimental implementation of QRAM with a superconducting quantum processor A research team from Zhejiang University has reported the physical implementation of a circuit-based, bucket-brigade Quantum Random Access Memory (QRAM) architecture on a programmable superconducting quantum processor. Detailed in Nature Physics, the experiment investigates a hardware interface designed to address the data-loading bottleneck that occurs when [...] The post Experimental Implementation of a Bucket-Brigade QRAM Architecture on a Superconducting Processor appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scientists identify the origin of noise in spin qubit quantum processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a crucial step toward practical quantum computers by pinpointing the sources of decoherence that plague spin qubit systems, which are among the most promising candidates for large-scale quantum processors due to their compatibility with existing semiconductor fabrication. Understanding and controlling noise is absolutely essential for quantum error correction to work effectively, as even the most sophisticated error correction codes can only handle errors below certain thresholds. The fact that spin qubits are now achieving fidelities that exceed requirements for surface code implementation is particularly exciting, as this puts them in serious contention with other leading qubit technologies like superconducting circuits. By identifying specific noise mechanisms, researchers can now engineer targeted solutions to push spin qubit performance even further, potentially accelerating the timeline for fault-tolerant quantum computing.

A spin qubit, in which quantum information is encoded in the spin state of an electron, is one of the most promising platforms for quantum computing. Spin qubits exhibit long coherence times and are compatible with advanced semiconductor manufacturing technologies. The leading implementation of spin qubits involves confined electrons inside quantum dots, a nanoscale semiconductor architecture that behaves like a controllable artificial atom. Recent advances have enabled high-fidelity operation of single- and two-qubit gates, exceeding the threshold required for certain surface code quantum error correction techniques.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Ferroelectrical Switching as a Probe of Quantum Damping in Magnetic Spin Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental challenge in quantum magnetism by proposing an elegant experimental method to detect quantum effects in magnetic damping, which have remained frustratingly difficult to observe despite decades of theoretical predictions. The team's approach using ferroelectric substrates to electrically switch magnetic interactions between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic states offers a practical pathway to control and measure quantum entanglement in magnetic systems. What makes this particularly exciting is the connection between readily observable magnetization dynamics and the underlying quantum entanglement, potentially opening the door to voltage-controlled quantum devices based on magnetic materials. This work bridges the gap between fundamental quantum many-body physics and practical quantum technologies, offering a new platform for exploring quantum effects in condensed matter systems.

While damped spin dynamics is important for the understanding of magnetic materials, clear signatures of \emph{quantum corrections} to the Gilbert damping mechanism remain elusive. We propose a route to distinguish quantum and classical Gilbert spin damping using ferroelectric control of a magnetic dimer. Ab initio calculations for dimers on ferroelectric substrates show that polarization reversal switches the inter-spin exchange between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic regimes. We formulate a magnetization-based diagnostic that relates magnetization traces to entanglement dynamics, which enables ferroelectrical on/off control of dimer entanglement. Material-informed quantum Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert simulations illustrate how the signature of magnetization dynamics can, in principle, be used to infer the existence of quantum Gilbert spin damping. This minimal and non-volatile platform connects first-principles modeling to experimentally accessible observables and provides a starting point for voltage-controlled quantum entanglement in magnetic spin networks.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Coherent versus stochastic error injection on a repetition-code logical qubit in superconducting hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a crucial real-world challenge in quantum error correction: the gap between theoretical predictions and experimental reality when dealing with different types of noise. While theory suggests that coherent errors (which maintain quantum phase relationships) should behave very differently from random stochastic errors in repetition codes, the IBM team found no such difference in their superconducting quantum processor experiments. The suspected culprit is frequency drift in the qubits themselves, which appears to scramble coherent errors into effectively random ones, highlighting how subtle hardware imperfections can dramatically alter error correction performance. This work underscores the importance of bridging theory and practice in quantum error correction, showing that even small, seemingly innocuous effects like qubit drift can reshape how errors propagate through quantum circuits.

The performance of quantum error correction (QEC) codes is limited by the underlying physical noise. Theoretical studies show that coherent and stochastic noise have different effects when performing QEC with either surface or repetition codes. We use the bitflip repetition code, realized in a transmon quantum processor, as a testbed to experimentally study the impact of injecting coherent versus stochastic errors on the logical performance. We adapt a scalable free-fermion simulator to simulate the experiments and we modify a subset sampling technique to efficiently sample stochastic noise in the quantum circuit. In the experiment, we do not observe the difference in logical fidelity predicted by simulation for either the distance-3 or distance-5 repetition codes. We hypothesize that this discrepancy could be explained by small drifts in qubit frequencies, which introduce phase-coherent noise that `stochastifies' the injected coherent errors. Our work contributes to advancing an understanding of how coherent errors affect experimental QEC.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Suppression of Quasiparticle Poisoning to $10^{-11}$ Levels in Superconducting Qubits via Infrared Shielding
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough tackles one of the most persistent challenges in superconducting quantum computing: quasiparticle poisoning, where unwanted energy excitations corrupt qubit states and destroy quantum information. By implementing sophisticated infrared shielding, the researchers achieved a staggering four-order-of-magnitude reduction in quasiparticle density to just 1.88×10^-11 per Cooper pair - the lowest ever reported - essentially creating an ultra-clean quantum environment where qubits can maintain coherence far longer. The work demonstrates that with proper engineering of the electromagnetic environment around quantum processors, initialization errors can be reduced to just 0.01%, a critical milestone for building the large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers that major tech companies are racing to develop. This represents a significant step toward solving the scalability problem that has long plagued superconducting quantum systems, showing that environmental control may be just as important as the qubits themselves.

Quasiparticle poisoning bottlenecks superconducting qubits, limiting coherence and the scalability of quantum processors. In this work, we systematically investigate quasiparticle poisoning in superconducting qubits under three infrared (IR) shielding configurations, ranging from a dedicated multi-layer design to a simplified implementation. By measuring quasiparticle-induced parity switching, we demonstrate a suppression of the switching rate by over four orders of magnitude via the implementation of improved shielding. In the best configuration, the rate decreases over time following cooldown and reaches 0.069$\,$Hz on day 34, corresponding to an anticipated quasiparticle density per Cooper pair of $1.88\times10^{-11}$. To our knowledge, this represents the lowest quasiparticle density reported in the literature to date. The remaining quasiparticle population is likely dominated by sporadic phonon bursts stemming from mechanical stress release in the on-chip films, as well as from the surrounding environment. The effective qubit temperature follows the phonon bath down to 17$\,$mK, enabling initialization errors of $\sim 0.01\%$ for 3$\,$GHz qubits. These results demonstrate that proper IR shielding and thermalization are essential for suppressing quasiparticle poisoning and enabling high-coherence, scalable superconducting qubit systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SEALSQ Broadens Quantum Computing and Post-Quantum Security Strategy
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement signals a notable shift in how cybersecurity companies are positioning themselves for the quantum era, with SEALSQ expanding beyond traditional post-quantum cryptography into broader quantum computing investments. The move reflects growing industry recognition that quantum-safe security will require deep integration across the entire quantum technology stack, from hardware to software to implementation protocols. While many companies focus solely on developing quantum-resistant algorithms, SEALSQ's multi-pronged approach through acquisitions could provide valuable insights into how quantum and post-quantum technologies will converge in practical deployments. This strategy represents an interesting bet that success in quantum security will require understanding and controlling multiple layers of the quantum computing ecosystem rather than just the cryptographic endpoints.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SEALSQ Corp (NASDAQ: LAES) (“SEALSQ” or “Company”), a company that focuses on developing and selling Semiconductors, PKI, and Post-Quantum technology hardware and software products, today updated on its strategic expansion into the full quantum technology stack through targeted investments and acquisitions across leading quantum computing companies. Building on its established […]

item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Companies Building Quantum Computing Chips in 2026
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article captures a fascinating moment in quantum computing where the field has matured beyond academic research labs but hasn't yet converged on a single dominant technology platform, much like the early days of personal computing when dozens of incompatible architectures competed for market share. The diversity of approaches being pursued commercially in 2026 reflects both the enormous technical challenges of building fault-tolerant quantum computers and the genuine uncertainty about which physical implementation will ultimately prove most practical for real-world applications. Each modality brings unique advantages and trade-offs, from the fast gate operations of superconducting qubits to the long coherence times of trapped ions, creating a rich competitive landscape that should accelerate innovation across all platforms. This technological pluralism is actually healthy for the field, as it ensures multiple pathways toward the holy grail of fault-tolerant quantum computing rather than betting everything on a single approach.

Insider Brief Quantum computing hardware in 2026 looks like several distinct technologies, each with different physics, different manufacturing constraints, and different ideas about what fault-tolerant quantum computing should look like when it arrives. Superconducting qubits, trapped ions, photonics, neutral atoms, and silicon spin qubits are all in active commercial development. No modality has established the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Experimental Demonstration of Free-Space Unidimensional Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution Under High Detector Noise
item.description:
Curator's Take: This experimental work tackles one of the most stubborn practical challenges in quantum cryptography: making continuous-variable quantum key distribution work reliably in free-space environments where detector noise typically kills the quantum advantage. The researchers achieved a respectable 270 kbps secure key rate using a simplified unidimensional approach that requires only standard telecom equipment, making quantum-secured communications more accessible than complex discrete-variable systems. However, the stark difference between trusted and untrusted detector noise models reveals a critical vulnerability - the system only works when you can guarantee your detectors haven't been tampered with, which significantly limits real-world deployment scenarios. This study provides valuable insights for the quantum communication community about the fundamental trade-offs between system simplicity, noise tolerance, and security assumptions in practical quantum cryptography implementations.

Continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD), which uses quadratures of the electromagnetic field, enables practical quantum communication using standard telecommunication technologies. Unidimensional CV-QKD (UD-CVQKD) simplifies the implementation by restricting modulation to a single quadrature. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate a free-space Gaussian-modulated UD-CVQKD system operating under a high detector electronic-noise regime (1.4 shot-noise units). The system employs polarized coherent states with signal and local oscillator co-propagating in the same spatial mode in orthogonal polarizations, ensuring stable interference. System security is analyzed under both untrusted (UTD) and trusted (TD) detector noise models. While no positive secret key rate is obtained under the UTD model, the TD model enables secure key generation over a finite range of modulation variances, highlighting the critical role of detector trust in high-noise conditions. A maximum secret key rate of 270 kbps is achieved at an optimal modulation variance of 11.57. Furthermore, secure operation requires high-transmittance (low-loss) channels under such noise conditions. This study demonstrates the practical feasibility of free-space UD-CVQKD in realistic high electronic-noise detection constraints and highlights detector electronic noise as a key limiting factor in practical systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scientists Identify the Origin of Noise in Spin Qubit Quantum Processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a crucial step toward building more reliable quantum computers by pinpointing the specific sources of noise that plague spin qubit systems. While spin qubits are among the most promising quantum computing platforms due to their compatibility with existing semiconductor manufacturing and relatively long coherence times, noise has remained a persistent challenge limiting their performance. By identifying exactly where this noise originates, scientists can now develop targeted mitigation strategies rather than taking a scattershot approach to improving qubit quality. This kind of fundamental understanding is essential for scaling spin qubit systems from laboratory demonstrations to the large-scale, error-corrected quantum computers needed for practical applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — A spin qubit, in which quantum information is encoded in the spin state of an electron, is one of the most promising platforms for quantum computing. Spin qubits exhibit long coherence times and are compatible with advanced semiconductor manufacturing technologies. The leading implementation of spin qubits involves confined electrons inside […]

item.title: Chinese Scientists Demonstrate Quantum Random Access Memory Architecture Aimed at Solving Data Bottleneck
item.description:
Curator's Take: This demonstration of quantum random access memory (QRAM) on a superconducting processor represents a crucial step toward making quantum computers practically useful for real-world applications that involve large classical datasets. The data bottleneck has long been one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges - while quantum processors excel at certain computations, they've struggled with efficiently accessing and manipulating the vast amounts of classical data that many algorithms require. By showing that QRAM can work on actual quantum hardware rather than just in theory, the Chinese team has opened a pathway for quantum computers to tackle problems in machine learning, optimization, and database search that were previously impractical due to data access limitations. This breakthrough could significantly accelerate the timeline for quantum advantage in commercially relevant applications that blend quantum processing with classical data.

Insider Brief Chinese researchers have experimentally demonstrated a quantum random access memory architecture, or QRAM, on a superconducting quantum processor, marking what researchers describe as a significant step toward improving how quantum computers access large amounts of conventional data. The work, published recently in Nature Physics by a team led by Zhejiang University and highlighted […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Classiq and UC Chile Launch Quantum Computing Research for Biomedical Imaging
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between Classiq and UC Chile represents a promising convergence of quantum computing with practical healthcare applications, specifically targeting the computationally intensive challenge of medical image analysis where quantum algorithms could potentially offer significant advantages. The project's hybrid approach, combining quantum algorithms with classical machine learning on NVIDIA's CUDA-Q platform, exemplifies the current industry trend toward near-term quantum-classical systems that can deliver practical benefits before fault-tolerant quantum computers arrive. Biomedical imaging is particularly well-suited for quantum enhancement given the complex pattern recognition and optimization problems involved in pathology analysis, making this a strategic research direction that could accelerate both quantum algorithm development and medical diagnostics capabilities.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Classiq and Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile (UC Chile) today announced a joint research project to develop hybrid quantum algorithms for biomedical image analysis, assisted by classical machine learning and the NVIDIA CUDA-Q platform for quantum-classical computing. The 12-month engagement, titled “Enhancing Pathology through Quantum Computing,” is funded through Avanza UC 2025, the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider

item.title: Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Sign Quantum Computing MOU
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership between Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric represents a significant step toward bringing quantum computing into real-world industrial applications, particularly in manufacturing and electrical systems where Mitsubishi Electric has decades of expertise. The collaboration could accelerate the development of quantum solutions for complex optimization problems that plague modern manufacturing, from supply chain logistics to energy management systems. What makes this particularly noteworthy is Mitsubishi Electric's practical engineering focus, which could help bridge the gap between quantum computing's theoretical promise and actual deployment in industrial settings. This type of industry-academia partnership is becoming increasingly crucial as quantum computing moves from research labs toward commercial viability.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantinuum, a leading quantum computing company, announced today that it has signed a non-binding Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with Mitsubishi Electric Corporation, a recognized global leader in the manufacturing, marketing, and sale of electrical and electronic equipment and systems, to establish a framework for a strategic partnership aiming at accelerating […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Projector Quantum Variational Ansatz
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research bridges a crucial gap between near-term NISQ algorithms and future fault-tolerant quantum computing by developing a variational ansatz that mimics the projector-based approach used in powerful FTQC algorithms like Quantum Phase Estimation. The work is particularly significant because it offers a potential pathway to access some benefits of fault-tolerant algorithms within current noisy quantum hardware limitations, while maintaining the shallow circuit depths that ADAPT-VQE provides. By creating a hybrid approach that can switch between ISQ-QSP and ADAPT-VQE structures depending on parametrization, this could help quantum computing researchers better prepare for the eventual transition from NISQ to fault-tolerant systems. This represents an important step toward making quantum algorithms more robust and scalable across different hardware generations.

Quantum computing offers several algorithms to compute the ground state of a problem Hamiltonian. The most desirable algorithms belong to the Fault Tolerant QuantumComputing (FTQC) regime, such as quantum algorithms with repetitive structure like Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) and Quantum Signal Processing (QSP). However, in the Noisy In-termediate Scale Quantum (NISQ) regime, the most realistic approaches involve Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) algorithms and their variants. VQE is an algorithm that searches for a parametrized unitary matrix called an ansatz whose purposeis to transform an easily prepared initial state into the groundstate of a given Hamiltonian. Adaptive Derivative-AssembledPseudo-Trotter (ADAPT)-VQE is a variant of VQE that im-proves this approach by constructing the ansatz iteratively so that the associated quantum circuit is as shallow as possible. A major difference between FTQC (i.e. not variational) algorithms and VQE is that FTQC algorithms do not construct a state transitiondirectly. Instead, they construct a projector that identifies the ground state using ancillary qubits that flag the good solution. The desired state is then obtained via amplitude amplification orpost-selection. In this work, we propose a VQE ansatz whose structure is more similar to that of an FTQC algorithm. Depending on its parametrization, this ansatz can be equivalent to either an Intermediate Scale Quantum (ISQ)-QSP or to an ADAPT-VQE quantum circuit structure. Our experimental results show that this first proposal of Projector Variational Ansatz (PVA) converges with a shallower ansatz than the usual ADAPT-VQE.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum correlations and coherence in a two-qubit anisotropic $XY$ under magnetic field
item.description:
Curator's Take: This theoretical study provides crucial insights for quantum hardware designers by mapping out exactly how different types of quantum correlations degrade under realistic conditions like temperature fluctuations and magnetic field variations. The research reveals a fascinating hierarchy where quantum coherence proves most resilient to thermal noise, outlasting even entanglement - a finding that could guide the development of more robust quantum processors that leverage coherence-based protocols. Particularly valuable is the identification of how magnetic anisotropy can prevent the abrupt "sudden death" of entanglement, instead allowing for smoother degradation that might be easier to manage in practical quantum systems. These results offer a theoretical roadmap for optimizing qubit designs and operating conditions to maximize quantum advantage in noisy, real-world environments.

We study thermal quantum correlations and coherence in Heisenberg $XY$ model with anisotropic interactions under a uniform magnetic field $ B $. Using concurrence $C$, local quantum uncertainty (LQU), Bell-Clauser-Horne-Shimony-Holt (CHSH) nonlocality $ \mathbb{B}$, and coherence $C_l$ as quantifiers, we analyze how magnetic anisotropy $ δ_m $, coupling anisotropy $ δ_c $, Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction $ D $, temperature $ T $, and magnetic field $ B $ modulate quantum resources. At low temperatures and relevant magnetic fields, the entanglement is maximized, but exhibits sudden death for $ δ_m = 0 $, which turns into a smooth decay as $ δ_m $ increases, highlighting its stabilizing role. LQU shows that stronger anisotropy suppresses quantum correlations, while $ \mathbb{B} $ induces a non-monotonic response peaking at a critical field $ B_c $. Bell-CHSH nonlocality violations ($ \mathbb{B} > 2 $) persist below $ B_c $, but thermal noise ($ T \geq 1 $) suppresses them. Coherence $ C_l $ is most robust to thermal fluctuations, especially for high \( δ_m \), which also dampens abrupt quantum phase transitions. The DM interaction is essential for entanglement generation, with $ D $ and anisotropy synergistically enhancing correlation resilience. We identify a hierarchy of thermal degradation: nonlocality ($ \mathbb{B} $) vanishes first, followed by entanglement ($ C $), then general quantum correlations (LQU), while coherence $ C_l $ persists the longest. These results demonstrate tunable control of quantum resources via anisotropy and external parameters, providing insights for the design of robust spin-based quantum technologies.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Exact noise characterization of entanglement distribution in star networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing practical challenges in quantum networking: understanding exactly how noise accumulates when distributing multipartite entangled states across star-shaped networks, which are expected to be the first scalable quantum network architectures. The work provides crucial analytical tools that allow network designers to optimize protocols without expensive simulations, addressing the fundamental problem that quantum memories decohere while waiting for all network links to be established successfully. By comparing factory and piecemaker distribution protocols and deriving exact noise characterizations, this study offers essential insights for building real-world quantum networks where timing uncertainties and memory limitations are unavoidable constraints. The mathematical framework developed here could significantly accelerate the development of practical quantum communication networks by enabling precise performance predictions and protocol optimization.

Multipartite entanglement forms the core of many networking applications. In the near-term future, it is expected that multipartite distribution will be achieved first through star topologies, making it important to understand the noise incurred during the distribution process. In such networks, elementary links are created stochastically and successful links must be stored while waiting for the remaining links, causing memory decoherence that depends on the random waiting times. We derive analytical expressions for both the average noise and its distribution, when distributing GHZ states under memory dephasing in star networks. We study and compare two distribution protocols: the factory and piecemaker protocol. Furthermore, we find expressions for the case of a global cut-off (allowing fast optimization of the cut-off without requiring Monte Carlo simulations) and extend the analysis for the factory protocol to depolarizing noise for arbitrary states.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Solution of the Equation-of-Motion Phonon Method eigenvalue problems on the D-Wave quantum annealer
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research demonstrates a clever workaround for one of quantum computing's most promising but currently impractical applications - solving the massive eigenvalue problems that arise in nuclear physics simulations. While the theoretically optimal Quantum Phase Estimation algorithm remains out of reach for today's noisy quantum hardware, these researchers successfully adapted D-Wave's quantum annealer to tackle real nuclear many-body calculations by reformulating eigenvalue problems as optimization tasks. The hybrid approach, which combines quantum annealing with classical deflation techniques to extract complete eigenspectra, represents exactly the kind of near-term quantum advantage we need to see - taking quantum hardware as it exists today and finding meaningful scientific applications. This work could open doors for quantum-assisted materials science and nuclear engineering calculations well before fault-tolerant quantum computers become available.

The solution of large-scale eigenvalue problems is crucial in nuclear many-body theory, where Hamiltonian matrices often reach extremely large dimensions. Quantum computing opens new perspectives for addressing such demanding problems. Although the Quantum Phase Estimation algorithm offers, in principle, a systematic route to matrix diagonalization, its practical deployment demands levels of coherence and error correction that current quantum hardware cannot yet support. A viable near-term strategy is instead to exploit quantum annealing, which enables the recasting of eigenvalue problems into quadratic unconstrained binary optimization formulations that can be addressed by existing annealing-based processors. Here, we propose a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm that combines quantum annealing and classical deflation to iteratively extract the full eigenspectrum of both standard and generalized eigenvalue problems. We benchmark this method on eigenvalue problems arising from the Equation of Motion Phonon Method performing calculations on real quantum hardware. Our approach illustrates the capabilities and limitations of near-term quantum devices in addressing nuclear eigenvalue problems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: OQC, JPMorganChase and AMD Commence Research Collaboration to Develop New Quantum-AI Platform in London
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration represents a significant step toward practical quantum-AI integration, with JPMorganChase becoming one of the first major financial institutions to test quantum algorithms in a dedicated enterprise-grade environment rather than relying solely on cloud access. The partnership combines OQC's quantum hardware expertise with AMD's classical computing infrastructure, creating a hybrid platform that could accelerate the development of quantum advantage applications in financial modeling and risk analysis. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the focus on building secure, on-premises quantum infrastructure in London, potentially establishing a template for how other enterprises might deploy quantum computing capabilities within their own data centers rather than depending entirely on external quantum cloud services.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — OQC, JPMorganChase and AMD today announced a research collaboration leveraging a new and dedicated Quantum-AI Data Centre, built by OQC in London. JPMorganChase researchers will test near-term quantum and hybrid quantum-classical computing applications via a secure enterprise environment to examine how quantum computing, AI and high-performance classical infrastructure can work […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nonreciprocal optomechanical entanglement in an asymmetric Fabry-Perot cavity
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research breaks new ground by demonstrating that asymmetric Fabry-Perot cavities can generate nonreciprocal quantum entanglement, where quantum correlations behave differently depending on direction - a quantum analog of how certain materials allow light to pass through more easily in one direction than the other. What makes this particularly intriguing is the surprising finding that classical nonreciprocity (directional transmission) and quantum nonreciprocity (directional entanglement) don't necessarily correlate as one might expect, suggesting these phenomena operate through fundamentally different mechanisms. The enhanced robustness of entanglement in asymmetric cavities compared to symmetric ones could prove valuable for quantum sensing and communication applications, where maintaining quantum correlations in noisy environments remains a major challenge. This work opens up a new research direction at the intersection of quantum optics and mechanical systems, potentially leading to novel quantum devices that exploit directional quantum effects.

Nonreciprocal transmission (classical nonreciprocity) in optomechanical systems based on asymmetric Fabry-Perot (F-P) cavities has been theoretically proposed and experimentally demonstrated. However, nonreciprocal quantum effects, particularly nonreciprocal quantum entanglement, remain unexplored in such systems. Here, we propose to generate nonreciprocal optomechanical entanglement in an asymmetric F-P cavity and discuss the connection between the nonreciprocal transmission and nonreciprocal quantum entanglement. We reproduce the nonreciprocal transmission spectra by solving the quantum Langevin equations, and then discuss the optimal parameters to achieve nonreciprocal optomechanical entanglement in the system. We show that a greater and more robust optomechanical entanglement can be approached in the asymmetric F-P cavities, in comparing with the symmetric cavities. Furthermore, we find that the degrees of classical and quantum nonreciprocities do not exhibit positive correlation as expected. Our work shows that the classical and quantum nonreciprocities can be realized simultaneously in the asymmetric F-P cavities, which provide a platform to explore the connection between classical and quantum nonreciprocities.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable Quantum Algorithms for Gutzwiller Projection
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical bottleneck in quantum simulation by developing a dramatically more efficient way to prepare Gutzwiller-projected BCS states, which are essential starting points for studying strongly correlated quantum materials like high-temperature superconductors. The amplitude amplification technique delivers a quadratic speedup over traditional measurement-based approaches, translating to a staggering seven-orders-of-magnitude reduction in computational overhead for realistic 100-site systems. While the projected-state weights still decrease exponentially with system size, this algorithmic breakthrough makes previously intractable quantum simulations of complex materials feasible on near-term fault-tolerant quantum computers. The work represents a significant step toward using quantum computers to solve some of condensed matter physics' most challenging problems, particularly in understanding exotic phases of matter where classical methods fall short.

Quantum simulation requires highly accurate input states. Gutzwiller-projected Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) states provide physically motivated input states for solving strongly correlated lattice models, but their preparation on a quantum computer is hindered by the non-trivial nature of the Gutzwiller projection. We construct scalable quantum algorithms for this task by combining a circuit construction for arbitrary BCS states with the amplitude amplification for Gutzwiller projection (AAGP) procedure. AAGP yields a quadratic reduction in the number of projection queries compared with measurement-based postselection and leads to substantially improved fault-tolerant resource scaling. For projected BCS states optimized for the square-lattice $t$-$J$ model, we find that the projected-state weight decreases exponentially with system size, but the quadratic improvement is still large enough at physically relevant finite sizes to make a decisive practical difference. In particular, for a 100-site benchmark, AAGP reduces the required number of projection queries by about seven orders of magnitude. These results establish AAGP as an enabling input-state preparation protocol for projected BCS states in quantum simulation.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: C12 Automates Pick & Place Nanoassembly to Standardize Carbon Nanotube Qubit Fabrication
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development marks a crucial step toward making carbon nanotube qubits manufacturable at scale, addressing one of the biggest challenges facing this promising quantum computing platform. C12's Pick & Place process cleverly separates the high-temperature nanotube growth from the delicate quantum circuit fabrication, potentially solving the thermal incompatibility issues that have plagued CNT qubit production. Carbon nanotubes offer unique advantages like naturally long coherence times and single-atom precision, but until now have been notoriously difficult to integrate into reliable manufacturing workflows. This automation breakthrough could position carbon nanotube qubits as serious competitors to superconducting and trapped-ion systems by making them economically viable for quantum processors.

Quantum hardware venture C12 has introduced Pick & Place, a patented nanoassembly process engineered to transfer single-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) onto pre-fabricated quantum circuits with micrometric precision. The fabrication method serves as a foundational manufacturing block to decouple high-temperature nanotube growth from sensitive, sub-micron chip lithography layers. item.title: Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Sign Agreement to Integrate Trapped-Ion Hardware into Industrial Design Lifecycles
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant step toward quantum computing's integration into real-world industrial applications, moving beyond academic research toward practical engineering workflows. By combining Quantinuum's high-fidelity trapped-ion systems with Mitsubishi Electric's established industrial simulation expertise, the collaboration could accelerate quantum advantage in complex manufacturing optimization problems where classical computers struggle with exponential scaling. The focus on integrating quantum hardware directly into existing design lifecycles is particularly noteworthy, as it addresses the critical challenge of making quantum computing accessible to engineers who aren't quantum specialists. While still early-stage with the non-binding MOU, this type of industry-quantum hardware partnership could serve as a template for bringing quantum benefits to traditional engineering sectors.

Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Corporation have signed a non-binding Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to build a joint development framework for advanced industrial engineering and manufacturing design. The partnership aims to combine Quantinuum’s high-fidelity trapped-ion hardware platforms with Mitsubishi Electric’s extensive industrial simulation libraries. Curator's Take: This article marks a watershed moment for the quantum computing industry as Quantinuum becomes one of the first major quantum hardware companies to go public, raising $1.68 billion in what represents the largest quantum computing IPO to date. The successful upsizing of their offering signals strong investor confidence in trapped-ion quantum computing technology and validates the commercial potential of quantum systems beyond just research applications. Quantinuum's public debut provides crucial capital for scaling their quantum hardware and software platforms while establishing a benchmark valuation that will likely influence funding decisions across the entire quantum ecosystem. This IPO represents a maturation milestone for quantum computing, transitioning from a purely venture-backed sector to attracting mainstream public market investment.

Quantinuum Inc. has finalized the pricing of its upsized initial public offering (IPO) on the Nasdaq Global Market under the ticker symbol QNT. The transaction structure issued 28,000,000 shares of Class A common stock at a public offering price of $60.00 per share, expanding the initial capital targets to accumulate approximately $1.68 billion in gross proceeds. item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A superconducting surface-code processor with lattice-surgery logical operations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates the first practical implementation of lattice surgery operations between surface-code logical qubits, marking a pivotal step toward truly scalable quantum computation. The team not only achieved fault-tolerant logical operations with impressive error rates below 4% per cycle, but also executed a complete quantum algorithm (Deutsch-Jozsa) entirely at the logical level using error-corrected qubits. Most significantly, they implemented magic-state injection to perform non-Clifford gates, which are essential for universal quantum computation but notoriously difficult to achieve fault-tolerantly. This work bridges the gap between theoretical surface code proposals and practical quantum processors, offering a clear roadmap for building large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computers using existing superconducting technology.

Fault-tolerant logical operations are fundamental for scalable quantum computation. Here, we report the experimental realization of lattice-surgery operations between a pair of distance-three surface-code logical qubits on a planar superconducting processor. During repeated syndrome extraction cycles, the logical qubits exhibit per-cycle error rates of $0.0365(2)$ and $0.0282(1)$, respectively, after leakage events are rejected. By leveraging joint initialization and lattice splitting, we deterministically prepare a logical Bell state, confirming genuine bipartite entanglement via the error-corrected logical state fidelity. We further execute a two-qubit Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm at the logical level to demonstrate algorithmic utility in a fault-tolerant framework. Finally, to achieve universal control, we implement magic-state injection and gate teleportation to realize continuous non-Clifford rotations about the logical $X$ axis. For the logical $R_{X}(π/4)$ gate, we achieve a logical gate fidelity of $0.943_{-9}^{+10}$ conditioned on the absence of detected errors. These results establish lattice surgery as a practical and versatile paradigm for logical computation in near-term surface-code architectures, representing a critical milestone toward scalable fault-tolerant quantum advantage in superconducting circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Demystifying Objectivity with Operator Algebra Quantum Error Correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article bridges two major quantum computing concepts - quantum error correction and quantum Darwinism - to provide a more rigorous mathematical foundation for understanding how classical information emerges from quantum systems. The key insight is connecting the abstract notion of "objectivity" in quantum Darwinism to the concrete algebraic structure of quantum error correction codes, particularly stabilizer codes that are widely used in quantum computing. This connection not only clarifies long-standing conceptual puzzles about the quantum-to-classical transition but also opens up practical computational tools for simulating how quantum information becomes classical through decoherence. The work could prove valuable for both fundamental quantum theory and practical quantum error correction schemes, as it provides coding-theoretic methods to analyze and classify different types of quantum-to-classical transitions.

Quantum Darwinism extends the decoherence formalism to explain how classicality and objectivity emerge from quantum mechanics. However, existing approaches often capture only partial aspects of objectivity, leading to its mischaracterization and making it difficult to pin down precisely. By connecting quantum Darwinism to operator algebra quantum error correction, we show that the emergence of objectivity can be identified with the algebraic local recoverability of quantum codes. Applying this algebraic framework to stabilizer codes, we show that it yields a far more precise characterization of classicality and redundancy, unifies the traditional measures of objectivity, enables efficient classification via coding-theoretic tools, and supports large-scale Clifford simulations of decoherence dynamics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Fun with Graph States: Nonlocal Bell Pairs and the Arf Invariant
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research unveils a surprisingly elegant mathematical structure underlying graph states, revealing that these widely-used quantum states can be decomposed into collections of nonlocal Bell pairs paired with unentangled ancillary qubits. The discovery that graph state properties are governed by fundamental algebraic concepts like the Arf invariant and adjacency matrix rank over finite fields provides new theoretical tools for understanding why measurement-based quantum computation works so effectively. This mathematical insight could lead to more efficient ways to prepare and manipulate graph states in quantum hardware, potentially streamlining protocols for quantum sensing and distributed quantum computing where graph states serve as essential resources. The work bridges quantum information theory with algebraic topology in a way that may unlock new perspectives on quantum advantage and inspire novel quantum algorithms.

We study inner products and partial amplitudes of graph states--a commonly employed class of quantum states, which are specified by graphs. We find that the magnitudes of these quantities are simply related to the rank of the adjacency matrix of the graph over F_2 while the phase is determined by the Arf invariant of its quadratic refinement. These facts motivate a nonlocal tensor factorization of the Hilbert space, with respect to which all graph states are products of Bell pairs with unentangled ancillae. These results may illuminate the quantum advantage in the framework of Measurement-Based Quantum Computation and suggest that graph states can be usefully visualized in the language of algebraic topology. In addition, we develop a specialized technique for computing expectation values of qubit-wise permutations in graph states, which is useful for calculating multi-invariants.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Breakeven demonstration of quantum low-density parity-check codes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research marks a crucial milestone in quantum error correction by demonstrating breakeven performance with quantum low-density parity-check codes, where logical qubits actually live as long as the underlying physical qubits - a key threshold for practical quantum computing. The team's achievement of encoding 4 logical qubits into just 18 physical qubits while maintaining error rates 9 times better than previous demonstrations shows the promise of qLDPC codes as more efficient alternatives to the widely-studied surface code. What makes this particularly impressive is the use of trapped-ion hardware's inherent flexibility to test nine different error correction schemes on the same device, proving these codes can work without exotic hardware modifications like long-range couplers that other platforms might require. The novel OMG architecture implementation that eliminates the need for ion shuttling could significantly streamline future trapped-ion quantum computers while making sophisticated error correction more practical.

High-rate quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes are a leading candidate for fault-tolerant quantum computing. They feature higher encoding rates than planar alternatives such as the surface code, but their implementation often entails significant hardware hurdles like the need for long-range couplers. We leverage the flexibility of a trapped-ion quantum computer to demonstrate nine quantum error-correcting codes with starkly different qubit connectivity requirements on a single device without any hardware reconfiguration. These experiments span three families of quantum error-correcting codes: qLDPC codes, topological codes, and concatenated codes. With a qLDPC code encoding 4 logical qubits into 18 physical qubits, we achieve a logical error rate up to $9\times$ better than a previous demonstration of a similar code on superconducting solid-state qubits. Moreover, our implementation exhibits breakeven performance, with some instances achieving qubit lifetimes comparable to or slightly exceeding that of our trapped-ion qubits. We use a novel implementation of the optical-metastable-ground (OMG) architecture for addressable mid-circuit measurement and reset, which enables us to perform these experiments without any ion transport or dedicated coolant ions, requirements that typically consume a large fraction of the runtime or ion count of trapped-ion quantum computers.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nanostructure modelling with early fault tolerant quantum computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research presents a compelling chicken-and-egg scenario where near-term fault-tolerant quantum computers could accelerate the development of better quantum hardware by simulating the complex multi-electron dynamics in quantum dots that are too difficult for classical computers to handle accurately. The authors provide realistic resource estimates showing that meaningful simulations of 4-8 electron systems could run on quantum computers with hundreds of thousands of physical qubits within reasonable timeframes, making this a prime candidate for early quantum advantage applications. What makes this particularly exciting is that improved understanding of quantum dot behavior could directly feed back into better spin-qubit designs and quantum sensing devices, creating a virtuous cycle where quantum computers help design their own next generation. The practical focus on semiconductor nanostructures also bridges quantum computing with established semiconductor manufacturing, potentially accelerating commercial quantum technology development.

Semiconductor nanostructures are central to many developing technologies. Notably, double quantum dots are especially important for semiconductor spin-qubit architectures, quantum sensing applications, and quantum-dot solar cells. Accurate modelling is highly desirable but conventional methods can struggle when dynamics involve more than two interacting electrons. In this work, we present a quantum simulation framework capable of addressing multi-electron double quantum dots. We adopt an efficiently scaling 1$^\text{st}$ quantised representation of the system and develop algorithms based on both Trotterisation and qubitisation. Incorporating insights from classical simulations enables us to produce resource estimates that are more realistic than those obtained from theoretical error bounds. Using a standard surface code model with physical noise at $10^{-3}$, our results indicate that the ground-state energy of four electrons in a double quantum dot can be estimated in approximately 24 hours using 226k physical qubits, or an eight-electron system in 3.4 days with 314k qubits (with runtimes falling dramatically when more qubits are available). We anticipate that incorporating very recent advances including dense surface code architectures (Low et al. arXiv:2605.30455) may reduce these costs significantly further. We conclude that early fault tolerant computers may prove to be valuable tools for designing mature-era quantum technologies.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics

QuantX Labs' focus on optical atomic clocks addresses critical infrastructure needs for telecommunications, GPS systems, and financial trading networks, where ultra-precise timing can translate directly into competitive advantages and revenue. The $5 million investment from Serendipity Capital signals institutional confidence that quantum sensing technologies are approaching market readiness, potentially offering investors more near-term returns compared to the longer development timelines typically associated with fault-tolerant quantum computers. This represents part of a broader trend where quantum sensing and metrology companies are securing significant funding as they bridge the gap between laboratory demonstrations and practical commercial applications.

Permanent capital firm Serendipity Capital has led the seed fundraising round for QuantX Labs, an Adelaide-based company specializing in precision quantum sensing and timekeeping. Serendipity Capital allocated US$5 million ($A7 million) into the equity tranche, marking the first external institutional financing secured by the hardware developer. item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Semidefinite-programming hierarchies for classically simulable state families
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental questions: when do quantum states actually provide an advantage over classical systems, and when are they just classical phenomena in disguise? The researchers developed a systematic mathematical framework using semidefinite programming to determine whether families of quantum states can be simulated classically, providing both computational tools to test for genuine quantum advantage and methods to find the optimal classical simulations when they exist. This is particularly valuable for understanding the boundary between quantum and classical resources in noisy quantum devices, where decoherence can wash out quantum advantages and leave systems that appear quantum but are actually classically simulable. The framework could help quantum algorithm designers identify when their protocols retain genuine quantum benefits despite noise, and guide experimentalists in determining the noise thresholds beyond which their quantum devices lose their computational edge.

Identifying whether a state family admits an irreducible quantum advantage is a fundamental task in quantum resource theory and quantum information processing. Here we study classically simulable state families, namely those residing within the convex hull of pairwise commuting families and therefore admitting a classical explanation. We develop a complete semidefinite-programming (SDP) hierarchy characterizing the set of classically simulable state families in arbitrary finite dimension. The key step is to reformulate classical simulability as a feasibility problem over deterministic response functions and auxiliary positive-operator-valued measures (POVMs) simulable by rank-one projective measurements. We establish a complete SDP hierarchy for rank-one projectively simulable POVMs and transfer the resulting characterization to state families, yielding both primal feasibility tests and dual affine witnesses certifying failure of classical simulability. Applying the hierarchy to state families mixed with depolarizing noise gives computable upper bounds on the critical classical visibility, which are matched by explicit classical simulations in several symmetric examples. These results provide a systematic convex-optimization framework for certifying classical simulability of quantum state families.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Risks Repeating Semiconductor and AI Mistakes Unless Government Acts Now on Quantum, Warns Cross-Party Parliamentary Group
item.description:
Curator's Take: This parliamentary warning highlights a critical inflection point for the UK's quantum ambitions, as the country that pioneered national quantum investment now faces the risk of losing its early-mover advantage without sustained government commitment. The cross-party concern echoes painful lessons from semiconductors and AI, where initial UK leadership gave way to dominance by other nations that maintained stronger, more coordinated public-private partnerships. With quantum technologies approaching commercial viability across computing, sensing, and communications, the timing of this alert couldn't be more crucial - the next few years will likely determine which countries capture the lion's share of what analysts project to be a massive quantum economy. The UK's decade-long head start and £1 billion investment could prove meaningless if competitors like the US, China, and EU continue ramping up their quantum spending while British support wavers.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The United Kingdom launched the world’s first National Quantum Technologies Programme in 2014, and has invested more than £1 billion over the past decade in building an ecosystem that spans quantum computing, sensing, communications and timing. According to analysis cited in the paper, quantum computing alone is projected to create […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Deployed trusted-node quantum key distribution over 300 km with a multi-core fiber access link
item.description:
Curator's Take: This demonstration represents a significant step toward practical quantum key distribution deployment by successfully operating QKD systems over Sweden's actual telecommunications infrastructure spanning 303 kilometers, including multi-core fiber segments that will be crucial for future quantum-classical hybrid networks. The researchers tackled real-world challenges by showing QKD can coexist with classical Ethernet traffic, handle dynamic channel switching between fiber cores, and operate through the high losses encountered in deployed fiber networks using advanced superconducting detectors. Perhaps most importantly, the team went beyond just proving the technology works to examine how limited QKD key generation rates affect actual encrypted communications, revealing that image transmission quality depends heavily on available key budgets and compression choices. This kind of end-to-end analysis of QKD performance in realistic network conditions provides invaluable insights for organizations planning quantum-secured communications infrastructure.

Quantum key distribution (QKD) is increasingly considered for deployment in realistic communication networks, where long distances, heterogeneous fiber infrastructure, and coexistence with classical traffic present substantial challenges. Here, we demonstrate trusted-node QKD between Linköping University and the Stockholm hub of the Swedish national quantum communication infrastructure over 270 km of deployed single-mode fiber, extended by a 33 km multi-core fiber (MCF) segment emulating a metropolitan access link, for a total distance of 303 km. The two sub-links use commercial QKD systems whose receivers are interfaced with external superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors, enabling operation at losses beyond those supported by standard internal gated-mode detectors. We operate the link while actively switching the QKD channel between two MCF cores, with co-propagating Ethernet traffic and injected broadband optical noise in the other cores. The results demonstrate the integration of commercial QKD into demanding, dynamically reconfigurable fiber infrastructure relevant to future hybrid quantum-classical networks. Finally, using the generated secret keys, we illustrate how limited and time-varying QKD throughput affects one-time-pad-protected image transmission: image fidelity depends strongly on the available QKD-generated key budget and the choice of compression algorithm, highlighting application-level challenges for QKD-based encryption in realistic scenarios.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
